text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing a clear peritoneum and liver without metastatic deposits or ascites. The greater omentum is retracted to expose the transverse colon, and the small bowel is positioned to the right side of the patient. The patient is placed in a steep head-down position with maximum right tilt to facilitate bowel movement to the right, exposing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and artery (IMA) for further dissection. A gauze is positioned to aid in retracting the bowel. The uterus is lifted and hitched to the abdominal wall, exposing the rectum and revealing the tumor.

The surgeon begins medial to lateral dissection to expose the inferior mesenteric artery, working in an avascular plane. The dissection continues into the holy plane of DME, revealing the hypogastric plexus and nerves. The inferior mesenteric artery is exposed, skeletonized, and clipped using Hemologs to secure the vessel. The inferior mesenteric vein is then clipped at its origin near the pancreas.
The surgeon continues the medial lateral dissection in the avascular plane, focusing on the lateral dissection without including the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon begins the resection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, starting with dissection in the posterior plane. The dissection is avascular, occurring between two nerves that are carefully preserved. Lateral and anterior demarcations are made to guide further dissection. The lateral dissection is completed, followed by anterior dissection, joining previously marked points. The left lateral dissection reveals a preserved nerve.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) with minimal bleeding, using traction and counter-traction techniques. Energy devices are applied at the lateral edges to manage bleeding from the middle rectal pedicle. The specimen is removed through a small incision with an Alexis wound protector, followed by an intracorporeal end-to-end colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler.
